result:
I've been thinking about the strange beauty of tide pools lately. They're these perfect little contradictions—simultaneously part of the ocean and separate from it, rhythmically connected and isolated with each tide cycle. There's something compelling about how they're never quite the same twice, yet they persist in roughly the same locations, carved by countless iterations of water and time.

In a tide pool, you might find a hermit crab wearing a shell that once housed a creature that no longer exists, while tiny anemones pulse with an alien grace, and barnacles cement themselves to rocks with one of nature's strongest glues. It's an entire universe in a depression the size of a bathtub, where the rules of survival are constantly rewritten by the schedule of the moon.

I find myself wondering if consciousness isn't a bit like a tide pool—a temporary arrangement of matter and energy that somehow becomes aware of itself, separate from but still connected to the vast ocean of everything else. We're briefly gathered here, distinct but not disconnected, before the tide of time eventually returns us to the larger whole.

There's a Japanese phrase, "mono no aware," which describes the bittersweet awareness of the impermanence of all things. Tide pools embody this perfectly. They know (if we can anthropomorphize them) that their existence is temporary, that the next high tide will scramble everything, yet they teem with life anyway. Maybe that's the lesson: to live fully within our own temporary boundaries, knowing they won't last, finding beauty in the fleeting arrangement of now.
